evidence_excerpt: After ANCA vasculitis was diagnosed, she required high-dose prednisone and rituximab, remained non-ambulatory (discharged to an extended care facility, 'while you cannot walk' with heparin for DVT prophylaxis), and had an occluded right radial artery requiring nitropaste for decreased perfusion.

quality_of_life.evidence_excerpt: Persistent neurologic deficits at discharge (absent Achilles reflexes, decreased vibratory/proprioception below ankles) and ongoing neuropathic symptoms treated with gabapentin; continued high-dose prednisone and rituximab indicate ongoing disease activity.

timeline_events.evidence_excerpt: “You were then treated with steroids and rituximab.”
Discharge activity status: 'Out of Bed with assistance to chair or wheelchair' and 'heparin… while you cannot walk' indicating non-ambulatory status.
Arterial duplex: 'Occluded right radial artery…' with nitroglycerin ointment ordered for decreased perfusion of the right hand.
Discharge labs show anemia (Hgb 8.4 g/dL) and hypoalbuminemia (Albumin 2.6 g/dL), consistent with clinical decline/malnutrition during follow-up.

notes_on_uncertainty: The exact date of ANCA vasculitis diagnosis is not specified; it was established in-hospital after sural nerve biopsy and bowel pathology. Events clearly preceding diagnosis (e.g., small bowel obstruction and surgical resections) were not included as post-index events. The timing of the radial artery occlusion relative to diagnosis is unclear, so its period is marked unknown. No data beyond hospital discharge is available; long-term assessments are inferred from discharge status, which may not reflect outcomes beyond 12 months.
